California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
August 13′  ■99■
Ann Mills
Comm■ss■on for
State Capitol′
Sacramento′  CA
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-91-344
Dear Ms. MiIls:
you have requested advice on behalf of the executive director
of the Commission for Economic Developnent with respect to the
application of the mass maiLing provisions of the Political 
Reform
Act (the 'tAct'r ) . r
OUESTI ON
oo the mass mailing provisions of the Act apply to state
boards and conmissions in the same nanner as the provisions apply
to other governnental aqencies?
CONCLUSION
The mass mailinq provisions of the Act are applicable to alL
state and locaI goveinmental agencies in Catifornia, including
state commissioni such as the Commission for Econonic DeveloPnent.
FACTS
The connission for Econonic DeveloPnent (CED) is considering
the possibility of publishing a newsletter which woul'd be
dist;ibuted in-quantitLes of over 2oo per nonth. The executive
director would iike to know if such a publication would be subject
to the mass rnailinq provisions of the Act-
1' 
Governnent code Sections 8l-000-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Conmission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
L8OOO, et seq. AI1 references to regulatj,ons are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of ReguJ.ations.
Econom■c Development
Su■te l■■4
95814
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804・0807 0 (916)322´ 5660


ANALY S T S
Secti,on 89oo1 states that rrno newsletter or other mass mail-
ing shall be sent at public expense.'r .A mass mailing is defined
as two hundred or more substantially sinilar pieces of mail.
(Section 8204r.5.) Applied literally, 
this section wouLd prohibit
aIl nass nailing involving public funds, irrespective of content
or purpose, including such items as tax notices, college
schLdules, sanple ballots and other nass mailings sent by govern-
ment agencies. Regulation 18901 clarifies that appl-ication of the
prohibition is linited to items sent which include references to
Llected officers affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the maiIing.2
The basic prohibition is set forth in subdivision (a) of
Regulation 18901- and is as follows:
(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b)3, a
mailing is prohibited by section 89001 if al-I of the
following criteria are met:
(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of employ-
ment or tusiness, or post office box. For purposes of
this subdivision (a) (1), the itern delivered to the
recipient must be a tangible iten, such as a videotape,
record, or button, or a written document'
(2) The iten sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated 
with
the aqencv which produces or sends the maiLinq, or
the aqencv which oroduces or sends the mailinq, and is
prepareA or sent in cooperation, consultation, co-
6rdination, or concert with the elected officer.
(3) (A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid
for with public noneys; or
(B) costs of design, production, and printing
exceeding S5o.oo are pa j.d with public moneys, and the
design. production, or printing is done with the intent
The conmission's authority to
absurd results of its Iiteral
Fair Politicat Practices Com.,
3 s,rbdi.ri=ion (b) of Regulation
Iinited excepti-ons to the general
(B) rncludes
other reference to the name, office, photograph, or
an elected officer affiliated !rith
2
the
interpret Section 89001 to avoid
application was upheld in Watson
(]-990) 2).7 cal.App.3d 1059.
18901 sets forth a series of
prohibition.


of sending the item other than as permitted by thj-s
regulation.
(4) More than two hundred substantially similar
items are sent, in a single calendar nonth, excluding
any iten sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any itern described in subdivision (b) .
Emphasis added.
Regulation 18901 is applicable to itens sent by any
governmental agency, but only Lirnits rnailings of the agency which
include the name, photograph or other reference to an elected
officer who is affiliated 
Lrith that agency.
The Act defines an agency as including "any state agency or
locaL governnent agency. t' (Section 82004.) I'State agencyrr is
defined as rrevery state office, department, division, bureau,
board and commission, and the Legislature. " (section a2049,
emphasis added. ) This definition would include cED, and,
therefore, the provisions of Regulation 18901 are applicable to
CED .
Based upon inforrnation previously provj'ded by your office, it
appears that the chair of cED and several of its commissioners are
elected state officers. 
(Sectj.on 82021. ) Elected officers who
are members of an agency are considered to be "affiliated" 
with
the agency. (Regulation 18901(c) (1) .)
Si.nce the netrsletter you describe would be sent to more than
2OO persons in a calendar month, it would be considered a mass
mailing. If it is going to be sent at public expense to persons
at their horne, office, place of enploynent or post office box, it
may not incLude the nane, office, photograph or other reference to
the etected officers affiliated with cED unless otherwise
perrnitted by Regulation 18901.
For your reference, I am enclosing a copy of Regulation 18901
together srith a general infornation sheet outlining the basic
prohibitions and exceptions.


If you have any questions, pLease contact me at (916) 322-
5901.4
s incere Iy ,
scott Hallabrin,
Acting ceneral counsel

Cornmission advice letters nay be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10C per page.

state commissioni such as the Commission for Econonic DeveloPnent.

boards and conmissions in the same nanner as the provisions apply
to other governnental aqencies?

CONCLUSION: The mass mailinq provisions of the Act are applicable to alL
state and locaI goveinmental agencies in Catifornia, including
state commissioni such as the Commission for Econonic DeveloPnent.
